content:
Page numbers feel cheap because the database pays the growing scan cost before returning the same tiny page.

7 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

LIMIT 50 OFFSET 0 is fast.
LIMIT 50 OFFSET 5,000,000 asks for the same 50 rows after making the database find and discard five million candidates.
The response size stays flat. The work does not.
This is why OFFSET pagination survives code review, passes early load tests, and becomes expensive only after the product succeeds. The query shape did not change. The distance to the requested page did.
The replacement is usually keyset pagination: continue from the last ordered value instead of counting past everything before it.
OFFSET Makes the Database Walk Past Your Success
PostgreSQL’s documentation is unusually direct: rows skipped by OFFSET still have to be computed inside the server, so a large offset might be inefficient.
Consider:
SELECT id, created_at, title
FROM posts
WHERE status = 'published'
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 50 OFFSET 5000000;The database cannot teleport to “page 100,001” based on the number alone. It must produce rows in the required order, skip five million, then return 50.
An index can make ordering and filtering much cheaper:
CREATE INDEX posts_published_created_id_idx
ON posts (created_at DESC, id DESC)
WHERE status = 'published';But an index does not make a huge offset free. The executor can walk the index efficiently and still walk millions of entries that the application throws away.
The exact plan depends on the database, statistics, predicates, selected columns, visibility, cache state, and index design. It may use an index scan, index-only scan, bitmap path, or another plan. The invariant is simpler: the skipped prefix is work.
This cost creates second-order effects:

• More CPU spent examining entries

• More buffer activity

• Longer connection occupancy

• Greater contention with useful queries

• Worse tail latency

• More opportunities for the plan to touch uncached data
Look, ten million rows is not automatically a large table for a relational database. A badly shaped access pattern is the problem. The row count merely makes that shape visible.
Run the actual query with:
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
SELECT id, created_at, title
FROM posts
WHERE status = 'published'
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 50 OFFSET 5000000;ANALYZE executes the query. Use it carefully on production, especially for statements with side effects or expensive workloads.
Compare early and deep pages. Inspect actual rows processed, timing, loops, buffer hits, and reads. Do not publish one laboratory timing as a universal metric. Query performance is workload-specific.
Page Numbers Also Drift Under Concurrent Writes
Performance is only half the problem.
OFFSET describes a position in a result set, not an identity. If the result set changes between requests, positions move.
Suppose a client fetches the newest 20 records. Before it asks for the next page, five newer records are inserted. OFFSET 20 now skips five rows the client never saw and fifteen it already saw. Depending on order and changes, results can be duplicated or missed.
Deletes cause similar movement in the other direction.
A transaction using a stable snapshot can provide a consistent view for multiple queries, but holding database transactions across user pagination is usually impractical. Users wait seconds or minutes. Connections and snapshots are not browser-session state.
The ordering itself must also be deterministic.
This is incomplete:
ORDER BY created_at DESCMany rows can share the same timestamp. SQL does not promise a stable order among ties unless you provide one.
Add a unique tie-breaker:
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESCPostgreSQL explicitly warns that LIMIT and OFFSET without an ORDER BY that constrains rows into a unique order can return unpredictable subsets. Different limit or offset values can even lead the optimizer to choose different plans and therefore different unspecified row orders.
No ORDER BY means no meaningful pagination.
A non-unique ORDER BY means pagination can remain unstable at tie boundaries.
OFFSET with a unique order is deterministic for one database snapshot, but it still drifts as rows are inserted, deleted, or updated between requests.
Keyset Pagination Turns Position Into a Boundary
Keyset pagination — also called seek pagination — does not ask the database to skip N rows.
It says: return the next rows after the final key from the previous page.
First page:
SELECT id, created_at, title
FROM posts
WHERE status = 'published'
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 51;Return 50 rows and use the extra row only to determine whether another page exists.
If the last visible row has created_at = '2026-07-18T12:00:00Z' and id = 731, the next query is:
SELECT id, created_at, title
FROM posts
WHERE status = 'published'
AND (created_at, id) < ('2026-07-18T12:00:00Z', 731)
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 51;PostgreSQL supports row-constructor comparison. For another database, verify tuple comparison semantics and index support; the equivalent explicit predicate is:
AND (
created_at < :cursor_created_at
OR (created_at = :cursor_created_at AND id < :cursor_id)
)The comparison direction must match the ordering direction. For ascending order, the boundary normally uses >. For descending order, it normally uses <.
The supporting index should align with equality filters followed by ordered cursor columns. For the query above, the partial PostgreSQL index shown earlier can work well when only published rows are needed. A general index might instead begin with status:
CREATE INDEX posts_status_created_id_idx
ON posts (status, created_at DESC, id DESC);Which index is better depends on query mix, status distribution, write cost, storage, and database capabilities. Every index accelerates some reads by charging writes and disk space. Verify with the real plan.
Keyset performance is tied to page size and boundary lookup much more closely than to page depth. The database can seek near the cursor in an ordered index instead of stepping past the entire preceding result.
It also reduces drift. Newly inserted rows before the cursor do not shift the boundary. The client continues after a concrete ordered key.
It does not freeze reality. If rows can change the ordered columns, records may move across the boundary. Choose cursor fields that are immutable where possible, or define the consistency semantics explicitly.
A Cursor Is an API Contract, Not Base64 Decoration
Do not expose a raw integer offset in a field called cursor. That changes the encoding, not the algorithm.
A cursor must carry enough information to reproduce the seek boundary and, when necessary, the query context:
{
"created_at": "2026-07-18T12:00:00.000000Z",
"id": 731
}Production APIs often encode this structure as an opaque token. Base64 provides transport encoding, not integrity or secrecy. If clients must not modify cursor state, sign it with an HMAC or use authenticated encryption.
Consider binding the cursor to:

• Sort direction

• Active filters

• Tenant or authorization scope

• API version

• Snapshot cutoff, if the product promises one
Validate cursors. Reject malformed, expired, mismatched, or unauthorized values with a clear client error. Do not concatenate decoded fields into SQL; use parameters.
A response can look like:
{
"items": [],
"page": {
"next_cursor": "opaque-token",
"has_more": true
}
}Avoid an exact total count on every page unless the product genuinely needs it. COUNT(*) over a large, filtered, joined result can become another expensive query. Options include an estimated count, a separately cached count, a count on demand, or no count.
Keyset pagination has trade-offs:

• It does not naturally support jumping directly to arbitrary page 4,217.

• “Previous page” requires a reverse query and careful result reversal.

• Changing sort options requires matching cursor logic and indexes.

• Complex nullable sort keys need explicit null ordering and cursor representation.
For search results where direct page jumps are essential, OFFSET may remain acceptable within a bounded depth. Another option is to cap accessible pages and encourage filters. Administrative exports should use streaming, server-side jobs, or chunked key ranges rather than a user interface that clicks through every page.
Migrate Before Deep Pages Become an Incident
You do not need a flag day.
Add a cursor endpoint or version while preserving the old page-number contract temporarily. Instrument offset depth and query latency. Find clients that request deep pages. They may be crawlers, export scripts, integrations, or a UI generating unnecessary prefetches.
A practical migration sequence:

• Define a unique, stable order.

• Build and validate the matching index.

• Implement forward keyset queries with parameterized predicates.

• Encode, validate, and optionally sign cursor state.

• Fetch page_size + 1 rows to derive has_more.

• Add contract tests for ties, inserts, deletes, invalid cursors, and filter changes.

• Compare plans and resource use at realistic data volume.

• Deprecate or cap deep OFFSET access.
Test timestamp precision across the database, application language, JSON serialization, and cursor decoding. Truncating microseconds in a cursor can collapse distinct boundaries. Including a unique ID tie-breaker helps, but only if the timestamp value round-trips exactly enough for the comparison.
For the database mechanics behind this migration — EXPLAIN ANALYZE, composite and covering indexes, keyset pagination, and common slow-query patterns—the SQL Performance Cheatsheet is a direct companion.
OFFSET is not forbidden. It is excellent for small, bounded datasets, internal screens, and shallow navigation where simplicity wins. The failure is pretending its cost is constant while the offset grows without a limit.
The page still contains 50 rows. The database should not have to walk past five million to find them.
To sharpen the same reasoning across SQL, backend design, Docker, Git, AWS, and microservices, Froquiz has 10,000+ questions and realistic Senior Dev Challenges that test decisions rather than memorized syntax.
